ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mezza platter; 'appetizer food product' is the closest defensible parent class. Composite dish comprising hummus, baba ghanoush, moutabbal, tzatziki, and Arabic salad.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated mezza platter code; A00LC (mixed dishes) is the closest defensible category anchor for a composite multi-component appetizer platter.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to mezza platter as a food entity; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard mezza platter serving is approximately 500 g, typically shared among 2–4 people as a starter, comprising portions of hummus, baba ghanoush, moutabbal, tzatziki, and Arabic salad.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Hummus, Baba Ghanoush, Tzatziki, and Vegetable Salad component references (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for a composite mezza platter. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted blend of USDA SR Legacy entries for hummus (FDC ID 173757), baba ghanoush (FDC ID 168411), tzatziki (FDC ID 2103697), and chopped vegetable salad components. Input values (energy 150 kcal, protein 5 g, fat 10 g, carbohydrate 12 g, sugar 1.6 g, sodium 240 mg, dietary fibre 3.0 g, calcium 40 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 3 mg, saturated fat 1.6 g, trans fat 0.0 g, vitamin C 8 mg per 100 g) are consistent with USDA component data for this dish type and are retained as the best available estimate.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite mezza platter. GI estimated at 28 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for hummus (GI ~6) and chickpea-based dips (GI ~28–36), weighted against the tzatziki (GI ~11 for yogurt) and fresh vegetable salad (GI ~15) components. The dominant chickpea-based components anchor the estimate at GI 28 (low). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (28) × available carbohydrate per serving (60 g from 500 g platter at 12 g/100 g) / 100 = 16.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 240 mg/100 g; 1,200 mg per full 500 g platter
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content of 240 mg/100 g (1,200 mg per full platter) approaches the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals; request reduced-salt preparation, limit pita bread accompaniment, and monitor portion size to keep total sodium intake within clinical targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~28), high dietary fibre 3.0 g/100 g, low sugar 1.6 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: The chickpea-dominant components provide a low glycemic index (~28) and 3.0 g dietary fibre per 100 g, supporting gradual glucose absorption; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total carbohydrate load from the full platter (~60 g per 500 g serving) and substitute refined pita bread with vegetable crudités or wholemeal alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat 1.6 g/100 g, heart-healthy MUFAs from olive oil and tahini, high dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The platter's olive oil and tahini base provides monounsaturated fatty acids that support favourable LDL/HDL ratios; saturated fat is low at 1.6 g/100 g and trans fat is negligible, making this a cardioprotective appetizer choice when sodium intake is managed and pita bread portions are limited.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (eggplant, tomato, bell pepper) present in baba ghanoush and Arabic salad
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Eggplant, tomato, and bell pepper are nightshade vegetables that may exacerbate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; those with active RA symptoms should request nightshade-free components (hummus, tzatziki, cucumber salad only) and monitor for joint flare after consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mezza Platter (also spelled Mezze or Meze) is a classic Turkish and Middle Eastern shared appetizer platter comprising multiple cold dips and salads served together, typically with pita bread or vegetable crudités. The standard platter includes: Hummus — a smooth, creamy dip made from blended cooked chickpeas, tahini (sesame paste), lemon juice, garlic, and olive oil; Baba Ghanoush — a smoky, silky dip made from roasted or grilled eggplant blended with tahini, lemon, and garlic; Moutabbal — a closely related eggplant-tahini dip, often richer in tahini than baba ghanoush; Tzatziki (Taztiki) — a cool, tangy yogurt-based dip with grated cucumber, garlic, dill, and lemon; and Arabic Salad — a fresh, finely chopped salad of tomato, cucumber, onion, parsley, and lemon dressing. The platter is served cold and is designed for communal sharing. Nutritionally, per 100 g the platter provides approximately 150 kcal, 5 g protein, 10 g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from olive oil and tahini), 12 g carbohydrate, 3.0 g dietary fibre, and 240 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 28 (low), driven by the chickpea and yogurt components. The platter is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and high fibre content, and is cardioprotective due to its MUFA-rich fat profile and low saturated fat. Sodium content requires monitoring for hypertensive individuals. The nightshade components (eggplant, tomato) may be a concern for those with rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is halal and vegetarian. It is a staple of Turkish, Lebanese, and broader Levantine cuisine and is widely served in Middle Eastern restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: Is mezza platter good for diabetics?
What dips are in a mezza platter?

confidence_reason: No single approved source provides a direct nutritional entry for a composite mezza platter. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for hummus, baba ghanoush, tzatziki, and vegetable salad, which are consistent with the input values. The composite nature of the dish and variability in preparation ratios limit confidence to medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods component entries for hummus (FDC ID 173757), baba ghanoush (FDC ID 168411), tzatziki (FDC ID 2103697), and chopped vegetable salad; no single approved-source entry exists for a composite mezza platter. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for these nutrients is not individually resolvable from USDA FoodData Central composite entries for this multi-component platter as of 2024: monounsaturated_fat_g (detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in composite estimate), polyunsaturated_fat_g (detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in composite estimate), potassium_mg (not reported in composite estimate), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (not reported in composite estimate), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (not reported in composite estimate), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (not reported in composite estimate), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (not reported in composite estimate), vitamin_b6_mg (not reported in composite estimate), vitamin_b12_mcg (not reported in composite estimate), folate_mcg_dfe (not reported in composite estimate), vitamin_d_mcg (not reported in composite estimate), vitamin_e_mg (not reported in composite estimate), phosphorus_mg (not reported in composite estimate), magnesium_mg (not reported in composite estimate), zinc_mg (not reported in composite estimate), selenium_mcg (not reported in composite estimate), water_g (moisture data not resolvable for composite platter from USDA FoodData Central as of 2024). Source consulted: USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/), accessed 2024-01-01. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace per USDA component data for olive-oil and tahini-based dips; no industrial trans fats are present in traditional preparation. 4. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database chickpea and hummus entries; no direct GI measurement for a composite mezza platter exists in the approved GI database. 5. Pita bread is not included in the per-100 g nutrient calculation; it is an accompaniment that adds refined carbohydrate and additional sodium. 6. The dish is labelled as Turkish in origin but is broadly shared across Levantine, Lebanese, and Middle Eastern cuisines; the Turkish designation reflects the input record's primary_cuisine field and the restaurant context (GrabFood Singapore). 7. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore. The romanized_local field contains the Turkish original 'Meze Tabağı'.
